---
title: "Talylor's theorem with remainder to order 2"
date: "2026-05-03"
--- 

**Lemma(Taloy's theorem with remainder to order 2).** Let $f: \mathbb{R}^2 \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^1$ is $C^\infty$, then there exist $C^\infty$ functions $g_{11}, g_{12},g_{22}$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$ such that 
$$
\begin{align*}
f(x,y) = f(0,0) 
& + \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}(0,0) x 
+ \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}(0,0) y \\
& + x^2g_{11}(x,y) + 2xyg_{12}(x,y) + y^2 g_{22}(x,y) 
\end{align*}
$$

*Proof.*  Trivially, $(p,m) = (0,0)$ is *star-shaped* in $\mathbb{R}^2$ and the line segement $p+t(x-p), 0<t<1$ and $m+j(y-m), 0<j<1$ lies in $\mathbb{R}^2$. So that $f(p+t(x-p), m+j(y-m))$ is defined.

By the chain rule,
$$
\begin{align*}
&\frac{d}{dt} ~ \frac{d}{dj} f(p+t(x-p), m+j(y-m)) \\ 
& = \sum (x^i-p^i) \frac{\partial f}{\partial x^i}(p+t(x-p), m+j(y-m)) \\
& + \sum (y^i-m^i) \frac{\partial f}{\partial y^i}(p+t(x-p),m+j(y-m)  
\end{align*}
$$

If we double integrate both sides with respect to $t$ and $j$ from $0$ to $1$, we get
$$
\begin{align*}
& [[f(p+t(x-p), m+j(y-m))]_{t=0}^{t=1}]_{j=0}^{j=1}\\
& =\sum(x^i-p^i) \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{\partial f}{\partial x^i}(p+t(x-p), m+j(y-m)) dtdj \\
& + \sum (y^i-m^i) \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{\partial f}{\partial y^i}(p+t(x-p), m+j(y-m) dtdj ~~ (1)\\ 
\end{align*}
$$

Let 
$$
\begin{align*}
& g_1(x,y)=\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{\partial f}{\partial x^i}(p+t(x-p),m+j(y-m)) dtdj\\
& g_2(x,y) = \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1}\frac{\partial f}{\partial y^i}(p+t(x-p), m+j(y-m)) dtdj\\
\end{align*}
$$

Then both $g_1(x,y), g_2(x,y)$ are $C^{\infty}$ and (1) becomes
$$
f(x,y)-f(p,m) = \sum(x^i-p^i)g_1(x,y) + \sum(y^i-m^i)g_2(x,y) ~~(2)
$$

In case $p=0, m=0$, and $n=1$, (2) becomes
$$
f(x,y) = f(0,0)+ xg_1(x,y)+yg_2(x,y) ~~ (3)
$$

Applying this lemma second time, that is to double differentiate $f(p+t(x-p),m+j(y-m))$ with repect to $t$ and $j$
$$
\begin{align*}
& \frac{d^2}{dt^2} \frac{d^2}{dj^2}f(p+t(x-p),m+j(y-m)) \\
& = \sum(x^i-p^i)^2 \big[\frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^{2i}} f(p+t(x-p),m+j(y-m)) \big]+ (y^i-m^i)(x^i-p^i) \big[\frac{\partial^2}{\partial x \partial y}f(p+t(x-p), m+j(y-m))\big]\\
& + \sum(y^i-p^i)^2\big[\frac{\partial^2}{\partial y^{2i}}f(p+t(x-p),m+j(y-m)) \big]+ (y^i-m^i)(x^i-p^i)\big[\frac{\partial^2 }{\partial x \partial y} f(p+t(x-p), m+j(y-m)) \big] ~~(4)
\end{align*}
$$

Similarly we can integrate (4) and obtain
$$
\begin{align*}
& g_{11}(x,y) = \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{\partial^2 }{\partial x^{2i}} f(p+t(x-p), m+j(y-m))dtdjdtdj \\
& g_{12}(x,y) = \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{\partial^2}{\partial x \partial y} f(p+t(x-p), m+j(y-m))dtdjdtdj\\
& g_{22}(x,y) = \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{\partial^2}{\partial y^{2i} f(p+t(x-p), m+j(y-m)) dtdjdtdj\\
\end{align*}
$$
for some $g_{11}, g_{12},g_{22}$ are $C^{\infty}$.


We can finally obtain the equaiton in the case of $n=2$
$$
\begin{align*}
f(x,y)& =f(0,0) + (x^2g_{11}(x,y) + xy(g_{12})(x,y)) + (xyg_{12}(x,y)+y^2g_{22}(x,y))\\
& = f(0,0)+ x^2 g_{11}(x,y) + 2xy (g_{12}(x,y)) + y^2 g_{22}(x,y)~~ \square
\end{align*}
$$
